design_philosophy.core_loop: Traverse prehistoric levels using dynamic weapon selection to defeat enemies and bosses, rescue kidnapped cavewomen
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Health decay over time (except boss battles) forcing forward momentum; weapon management and selection between encounters
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Prehistoric caveman action hero wielding fantastical weapons (boomerangs, fire, electricity, stone wheels) against dinosaurs and rival tribes
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: SNES version heavily reformulated from arcade: introduces overworld map level selection (non-linear progression), extends levels, adds bonus stages, removes weapon charging mechanics, restricts weapon variety. Creates accessibility through choice while maintaining arcade action-run-and-gun DNA. Censorship-driven design: removed suggestive opening scene and replaced it with comedic hygiene narrative in international versions.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system with continue mechanics; Japanese version allows stealing lives from co-op partner when depleted
difficulty.continues_system: Continues available; international versions removed inter-player life theft mechanic present in Japanese release
difficulty.checkpointing: Health decay system serves as tension mechanic rather than traditional checkpoints; bosses provide safe damage windows
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Level selection via overworld map in SNES version allows players to choose route and difficulty pacing. Multiple ending paths based on boss battle route choices create adaptive difficulty perception.

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon pickup system: boomerangs, bones, fire, flints, electricity, stone wheels, clubs. Arcade and some ports allow weapon charging; SNES version removes charging mechanic. Weapons vary by level encounter.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Overworld map in SNES version allows non-linear level selection (unlike arcade forcing sequential play). Boss defeats unlock new routes; red and blue stage distinctions suggest conditional access (red stages replayable, blue stages one-time only per debug notes).
replayability_hooks: Multiple route selection at boss battle endpoints
Three different final boss endings based on chosen path
Two-player cooperative mode with competitive damage mechanics
Alternative hidden ending (credits easter egg with controller input check)

world_building_techniques: Opening cutscene establishing caveman vs. rival tribe conflict and kidnapped women rescue objective
Regional narrative variation: Japanese version uses darker 'sexual assault implication' framing; international versions reframe as comedic hygiene-driven escape
Overworld map visual theming of prehistoric environments
Boss character variety (Tyrannosaurus Rex in first level) implying escalating threat hierarchy
Multiple ending sequences based on player route choices, suggesting narrative branching

boss_design.philosophy: Punctuation encounters that pause health decay mechanic and offer safe damage windows. Route selection creates variable boss encounters. Different final boss exists in SNES version compared to arcade.
notable_bosses: Tyrannosaurus Rex (Level 1 encounter, establishes dinosaur threat)
Final Boss (varies by chosen route and version; SNES has unique variant different from arcade)

faction_indicators: Joe (green-haired protagonist) vs. Mac (blue-haired protagonist) as distinct caveman faction
Rival kidnapper tribe as antagonist faction
Kidnapped cavewomen as objective/resource to liberate
danger_signals: Dinosaur enemies (Tyrannosaurus Rex) as apex predator threats
Health decay timer creating resource scarcity tension
Boss battle checkpoints as escalating threat indicators
environmental_storytelling: Prehistoric level design establishing prehistoric setting and hazards
Weapon placement and variety across levels suggests different survival challenges per region
Overworld map visual progression from starting territory through expanding dangerous zones

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Joe & Mac (SNES) article
